text: Good morning. I want to thank SAGES for giving me the opportunity to present here.
My name is Brandon Andrew. I'm an MIS fellow at the University of Pittsburgh Medical Center.
Today I'll be presenting super mesenteric artery syndrome following gastric bypass.
We present a case of a thirty-three-year-old female, twelve weeks pregnant at presentation, who presented with two days of epigastric abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting.
Her surgical history is significant for gastric bypass in two thousand fourteen.
She did require a return to the operating room for over somewhere GJ staple line bleed shortly after her procedure.
She had significant weight loss BMI declined from fifty to twenty six which is a ninety two pound weight loss for her.
On exam she presented with upper abdominal distention and mild tenderness.
An MRI was obtained and that showed severely distended gastric remnant and duodenum.
We elected to undergo diagnostic laparoscopy with plan for G-tube placement into the gastric remnant.
The video shows the severely dilated gastric remnant, visibly distended within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon identifies and divides adhesions involving the BP limb, using energy to separate a thick adhesion at the proximal portion of the limb.
There was some improvement of the third portion of the duodenum.
The surgeon divides adhesions involving the rullum, using surgical instruments to separate the fibrous tissue from surrounding structures.
The surgeon places a G-tube into the visibly dilated gastric remnant during the diagnostic laparoscopy.
The patient tolerated PO intake for several days. However, she began to have persistently elevated G-tube output and ultimately recurrence of nausea and vomiting with PO intake.
A contrast study is performed through the G-tube, revealing limited outflow through the duodenum. A subsequent CT scan shows narrowing between the aorta and superior mesenteric artery (SMA) vessels.
The surgeon measures the duodenal diameter at the affected location, noting it is five millimeters, which visually suggests superior mesenteric artery syndrome.
The surgical team prepares the patient for diagnostic laparoscopy and gastroduodenoscopy through the remnant G-tube, with the intention to perform a duodenal jejunostomy.
The surgeon replaces the ports at their previous positions and performs lysis of adhesions. The three limbs of the gastric bypass are visible in the surgical field.
The video shows the third portion of the duodenum, which appears visibly distended.
The surgeon exposes the duodenum by dividing the vascular aspect of the transverse mesocolon, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon performs a Kocher maneuver on the duodenum, revealing the underlying inferior vena cava.
The surgical team completes the sectioning process, reaching the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) vessels, which are now fully exposed.

A pediatric endoscope is inserted through the gastrostomy and advanced into the duodenum. The endoscope navigates to the third portion of the duodenum, where an area of narrowing is observed, along with visible transmitted pulsations from the superior mesenteric artery.
This is seen here. This confirmed our diagnosis of SMA syndrome.
The surgeon performs a duodenal jejunal anastomosis using the common channel jejunum, placing an anchoring suture to secure the connection.
The surgeon creates enterotomies and performs a stapled anastomosis between the intestinal segments.
The surgeon approximates the edges of the common enterotomy using interrupted sutures, followed by a stapled closure to secure the tissue.
The surgeon places an anti-obstruction stitch near the anastomosis site and positions a drain in the area to manage fluid accumulation.
Postoperative KUB with contrast showed no gastric remnant dilatation and flow of contrast through the anastomosis.
Postoperatively, the patient did very well, tolerated PO intake without difficulty and had minimal G-tube output. The G-tube was removed in six weeks.

Superior mesenteric artery syndrome is seen in patients with dramatic weight loss as can be seen after a gastric bypass. These patients can lose one hundred percent or more of their excess body weight. These patients present with nonspecific symptoms of postprandial nausea and epigastric pain.

CT can demonstrate an aorta mesenteric angle less than fifteen degrees or duodenal diameter at the estimate of less than eight millimeters. Surgical options can include division of the ligament of trites or more definitive surgical bypass such as duodenal jejunostomy.

Thirteen cases of SMA syndrome have been reported in literature after gastric bypass. These patients typically undergo an extensive workup with non-diagnostic radiographic imaging and often operative exploration for other diagnoses prior to making the diagnosis of SMA syndrome.

And so surgeons should have a high index of suspicion to diagnosis of rare complication in patients who have substantial weight loss after gastric bypass surgery and present with recurrent intermittent obstructive symptoms that cannot clearly be attributed to another source. Thank you.
Sir, thank you, that was great. Really interesting case. And just a quick question.
